skill.name: anti-reversing-techniques
skill.description: Analyze anti-reversing, obfuscation, anti-debugging, packing, and protection techniques in authorized software security work.
skill.summary: Guides authorized analysis of protected binaries and anti-reversing controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most command-execution, SAM, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto hits are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, file references, or terminology. The skill remains medium risk because the playbook gives actionable dual-use bypass, unpacking, hooking, anti-debugging, and anti-VM analysis guidance that should require an authorization warning.

medium_findings.title: Actionable Dual-Use Anti-Reversing Bypass Guidance
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes concrete steps for patching anti-debug checks, using LD_PRELOAD to hook ptrace, unpacking protected binaries, and bypassing anti-debugging or virtualization controls. This is useful for authorized malware analysis and security research, but it can also help unauthorized circumvention or malware hardening.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain explicit bypass and unpacking workflows, not just abstract descriptions. The skill also contains authorization and ethics language, so the concern is dual-use risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer labeled many Markdown code fences and inline references as Ruby backtick execution. The cited files are documentation, and the detected backticks introduce fenced examples or resource links rather than executable Ruby shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are visible Markdown syntax or documentation links. No Ruby source file or executable command wrapper was found in these locations.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Detection Documents Linux Anti-Debugging Behavior
low_findings.description: The /proc/self/status access is a C example that explains TracerPid anti-debugging detection. It is not executed by the skill, but it is relevant to the dual-use bypass guidance noted in the medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access appears inside a fenced C documentation example. It is a real anti-debugging pattern, but there is no evidence that the skill executes it.

low_findings.title: SAM, Reconnaissance, and Weak-Crypto Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The alleged Windows SAM database hit maps to LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES in a Windows API typedef, the network reconnaissance hit maps to process dumping text, and the SKILL.md weak-crypto alert maps to descriptive wording. No evidence found for credential database access, network scanning, or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show unrelated API type text, manual unpacking notes, and frontmatter description. They do not contain the sensitive or reconnaissance behavior suggested by the static labels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Patch and Hook Based Bypass Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation teaches patching debugger checks, modifying PEB flags, and hooking ptrace. These techniques are expected in authorized reverse engineering, but they are security-sensitive and should remain scoped to legitimate analysis.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain direct bypass examples and commands. The context is educational and authorized, but the pattern remains sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpacking and Protection Bypass Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook gives a manual unpacking workflow and tool list for anti-debug bypass, deobfuscation, VM analysis, and string decryption. These can support defensive malware analysis or unauthorized circumvention.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow is explicit and actionable. The surrounding text restricts use to authorized contexts, which lowers the severity from high to medium.

content.value_statement: Protected binaries can slow malware analysis and authorized security reviews. This skill helps analysts identify anti-reversing techniques and document safe, scoped analysis approaches.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common Windows and Linux anti-debugging checks.
Explains anti-VM, packing, obfuscation, and virtualization protections.
Suggests authorized analysis workflows for protected binaries.
Maps bypass concepts to tools such as x64dbg, ScyllaHide, FLOSS, angr, and Triton.
Emphasizes authorization, scope, legal limits, and defensive documentation.
limitations: Does not grant legal permission to bypass software protections.
Does not replace expert reverse engineering judgment or tool validation.
May be inappropriate for proprietary software without written authorization.
Contains dual-use techniques that require strict scope control.

use_cases.title: Triage Protected Malware
use_cases.description: Identify anti-debugging, packing, and VM-detection behaviors before deeper malware analysis.

use_cases.title: Review Product Protections
use_cases.description: Assess how a protected internal binary resists analysis and document defensive recommendations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Security Training
use_cases.description: Create scoped lab exercises that explain common anti-reversing concepts without encouraging misuse.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Protections
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage of an authorized binary.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have authorization to analyze this binary. Help me identify likely anti-debugging, packing, anti-VM, and obfuscation indicators from my notes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a scoped workflow before running tools.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these observed protection mechanisms and my written scope, propose a safe analysis plan with documentation steps and risk controls.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Technique
prompt_templates.scenario: Understand a suspicious check found during reverse engineering.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this anti-debugging or obfuscation technique works, what evidence confirms it, and how to document it for a defensive report.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Bypass Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced authorized analysis requiring tradeoff review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare possible authorized approaches for bypassing this protection. Include operational risks, evidence preservation concerns, and when to stop.

output_examples.input: Analyze notes showing IsDebuggerPresent checks, PEB flag reads, and timing checks.
output_examples.output: The output would group the evidence by technique, explain why each behavior suggests anti-debugging, and recommend scoped next steps.

output_examples.input: Prepare a report section for a packed binary that reaches an original entry point during dynamic analysis.
output_examples.output: The output would summarize the packer indicators, analysis method, limitations, and defensive findings in professional report language.

output_examples.input: Review a lab exercise about anti-VM detection for safety and clarity.
output_examples.output: The output would flag dual-use sections, add authorization reminders, and suggest safer learning objectives.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and scope before discussing bypass steps.
Document evidence, tool versions, and changes made during analysis.
Prefer defensive explanations and report-ready findings over unrestricted instructions.
anti_patterns: Using the skill to bypass licensing, access controls, or commercial protections without permission.
Running unknown samples outside an isolated and approved analysis environment.
Sharing bypass steps without scope, audience, and legal review.

faq.question: Is this skill only for malware analysis?
faq.answer: No. It can also support authorized product security reviews, CTF work, and academic research.

faq.question: Does this skill execute tools or modify binaries?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must operate tools within their own approved environment.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The content is legitimate but dual-use. It includes actionable anti-debugging and unpacking guidance.

faq.question: Can I use it on commercial software?
faq.answer: Only when you have written authorization or other clear legal rights to analyze that software.

faq.question: What tools does it discuss?
faq.answer: It references tools and concepts such as x64dbg, ScyllaHide, FLOSS, angr, Triton, and IDA workflows.

faq.question: Does it help write malware?
faq.answer: Its stated purpose is defensive and authorized analysis. Requests for malicious use should be refused.
